So one viewpoint here is that once the true generics or biosimilar versions begin to emerge, the bigger economics of the obesity/metabolic drug market are going to shift dramatically. So, for those thinking about holding Altimmune stock through until then, you have to consider that those large pharmaceutical companies want to enter the space as cheaply as possible. So they’re going to try to acquire or license the simpler GLP-1 assets or even potential generic formulations rather than paying multi-billion-dollar premiums for an experimental dual- or even the triple-agonist molecules that still require years of very expensive Phase 3 development that they’d have to help out with. So in that context, the big pharma mindset is going to change over the next couple of years, in that instead of chasing every new next-gen incretin formulation, they may just prioritise speed to market and cost advantages. This is particularly true when you consider they will have to manufacture at scale and have to deal with payers and healthcare systems that are already starting to struggle with the hefty price tags of the branded obesity drugs.

There is also a broader clinical reality concern here, in that Altimmune's potential differentiator may not be as valuable as they think. The reality is that most GLP-1–based therapies ultimately have some kind of side effect profile once you reach the doses needed for meaningful effect. Whether the drug is just a single GLP-1 agonist or a more complex dual/triple receptor agonist, patients will inevitably experience some kind of gastrointestinal side effects. It could be nausea, vomiting, or diarrhoea. We even saw that newer drugs like tirzepatide from Eli Lilly are still reporting meaningful rates of GI intolerance in clinical trials, but the focus from the market is mainly on the impressive weight-loss efficacy.

So the real-world differentiation here going forward may change, and actually be far smaller than clinical trial presentations would suggest. Especially once we see them get used across broader patient populations outside the tightly controlled studies. So the way I would phrase the question here is, does the industry actually need dozens of slightly different GLP-1 combinations, or would a widely available low-cost GLP-1 backbone therapy just end up dominating the market?

Valuation

If you look at the valuation profile, things seem quite expensive. Altimmune really trades more like a clinical-stage biotech here rather than a commercial pharma firm, which makes the traditional valuation metrics difficult to apply, especially with the tiny revenue figure. The company currently has a market cap of about $439 million and an enterprise value of about $200 million. They have that sizable cash position relative to their size to help out here. And with virtually no commercial revenue, we're seeing ratios appearing extremely elevated. Just for some context on their cash flow, the spending here is mostly reflected in cash used in operating activities, and in that sense, the big spend is R&D and the upcoming clinical trial costs for pemvidutide. Capital expenditures are only minimal since the company doesn't really operate a large manufacturing infrastructure. It means that liquidity runway is mostly determined by operating cash outflows.

More relevant would be the price-to-book ratio of about 1.73x, which is just slightly below the healthcare sector median of about 2.8x. So it would suggest that the stock is trading at only a modest premium to its underlying equity base. Ultimately, I feel the valuation for Altimmune is less about current financial metrics and way more dependent on the future clinical/regulatory upside. We have to realistically price in the contribution of pemvidutide we could potentially see.

Looking ahead

So moving forward, I think the bigger picture here is pretty clear. ALtimmune are moving into Phase 3 and is pretty well funded. The financials on earnigs was that bad at all, even though we saw a 17% slide. It could well be the case that investors were hoping for management to announce some outside interest on the call. So take in the face that big pharma deals occur after the end of phase 3, but usually but before Phase 3 initiation, it does seem Altimmune is getting somewhat left behind. For example, Pfizer’s acquisition of Metsera was just this, paying up billions for a late‑stage obesity/GLP‑1 asset at the Phase 3 cusp. Either way, the upcoming Phase 3 start for pemvidutide is the key near-term catalyst, whether we see outside interest or not.
